title: The Honeymoon Phase of Kundalini: Why It Feels Amazing Before It Feels Terrible
meta_description: You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, scrolling in the dark, heart thumping, a cold sweat maybe, wondering what the hell is happening to you. I know that
body:
The Honeymoon Phase of Kundalini: Why It Feels Amazing Before It Feels Terrible

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, scrolling in the dark, heart thumping, a cold sweat maybe, wondering what the hell is happening to you. I know that feeling. I've been there. You're trying to figure out why something that started out feeling like pure bliss has turned into a living nightmare. You thought you were waking up, becoming enlightened, finally getting it. Now you just feel like you're losing your mind. You're not alone. Not by a long shot. What you're likely experiencing is the flip side of what they call the kundalini honeymoon phase.

See, kundalini isn't just some abstract spiritual concept. It's a real, powerful energetic process that can kick off in anyone, anytime. Sometimes it's triggered by years of spiritual practice, sometimes by trauma, sometimes by drugs, sometimes for no damn reason you can pinpoint. And when it starts, it can feel incredible. Truly. It's like the universe just opened up and poured pure joy into your being. You feel connected, vibrant, alive in a way you never have before. But then... it changes. And that's what we need to talk about.

The Initial Rush: Why It Feels So Damn Good

So, you're probably remembering those first few weeks or months. That's the honeymoon, right? That's when you thought you'd found the secret. You felt energy coursing through you, maybe you saw lights, heard sounds, had incredible insights. You felt like you were finally seeing the world clearly, like a veil had been lifted. You were probably buzzing with an energy you couldn't explain. You felt incredibly optimistic, maybe even invincible. Does that sound familiar? I bet it does.

This initial surge is often described as a burst of pure life force. It’s like your system gets a massive upgrade overnight. You might feel:

  • Intense Bliss and Euphoria: Like you're high on life, constantly. Everything feels vibrant and beautiful. You might laugh for no reason, just because being alive feels so good.
  • Increased Sensitivity: Colors seem brighter, sounds clearer, food tastes better. You feel more connected to nature, to other people, to everything. You're perceiving the world with new senses, right?
  • deep Insights and Clarity: Ideas just pop into your head. You understand complex concepts effortlessly. You feel like you're tapping into universal knowledge. You might even feel like a genius.
  • Physical Sensations: Tingling, vibrations, heat, coolness, energy surges moving through your body. Sometimes it feels like an internal massage, sometimes like electricity. It's all very new and exciting.
  • Deep Sense of Connection: You feel one with everything. The illusion of separation dissolves, and you feel like part of a greater whole. It's a powerful, unifying experience.

You're thinking, "This is it! This is enlightenment! I've arrived!" And honestly, who wouldn't? It's a taste of what's possible, a glimpse behind the curtain. It's like your nervous system just got a massive jolt of pure, unadulterated consciousness. And for a while, it's absolutely glorious. You feel lighter, more loving, more compassionate. The world seems full of possibility. You're probably telling everyone you know about it, trying to describe the indescribable. You're convinced you've found the answer to everything. And in a way, you have. But it's not the answer you thought it was.

The Trap of Expectation

Here's the thing: you probably started to expect that feeling to last forever. You thought this was the new normal. You started chasing it, right? Trying to recreate that initial high. That's a natural human reaction. We want to hold onto what feels good. But kundalini isn't about feeling good all the time. It's about clearing out everything that's not you, everything that's blocking that pure energy. And that's where the honeymoon ends and the real work begins.

I remember my own honeymoon phase. I felt like I was floating. Colors were so vivid, music was an ecstatic experience. I felt like I could read minds, like I was plugged into some cosmic network. I lost all fear. I thought, "This is what spiritual masters talk about!" I thought I was special. I thought I was done. Boy, was I wrong. That feeling, that initial rush, it's a lure. It gets your attention. It shows you what's possible. But it's not the destination. It's just the invitation to the journey, and the journey is often brutal. Know what I mean?

When the Wheels Come Off: The Shift to Crisis

And then, it flips. The bliss starts to fade, or it gets punctuated by intense, unsettling experiences. The beautiful sensations turn into painful ones. The clarity turns into confusion. The connection turns into overwhelming sensitivity and isolation. This is where most people, including me, start to panic. You go from "I'm enlightened!" to "I'm losing my damn mind!" in what feels like overnight.

What's happening is the energy, which was initially just flowing, starts to hit blockages. It starts to clean house. And cleaning house isn't always pretty. It means digging up all the old trauma, the repressed emotions, the limiting beliefs, the physical toxins you've stored in your body for years. It's like a cosmic pressure washer cleaning out your entire system, and every bit of gunk that comes loose feels like absolute hell.

You might experience:

  • Intense Anxiety and Fear: Panic attacks out of nowhere. A constant feeling of dread. You feel like you're dying, like something terrible is about to happen, even when nothing is.
  • Depression and Despair: A deep sadness that seems to have no origin. Feeling completely hopeless, empty, disconnected. The world loses its color.
  • Physical Pain and Symptoms: Burning sensations, electric shocks, muscle spasms, tremors, headaches, digestive issues, chronic fatigue. It feels like your body is breaking down, and doctors can't find anything wrong. They tell you it's "all in your head."
  • Emotional Volatility: Extreme mood swings. Crying spells, sudden anger, uncontrollable laughter followed by deep despair. You feel like you're on an emotional rollercoaster you can't get off.
  • Sensory Overload: Lights are too bright, sounds are too loud, crowds are overwhelming. You can't filter anything out, and it's exhausting.
  • Psychological Distress: Derealization, depersonalization. Feeling like you're not real, or the world isn't real. Disturbing visions, intrusive thoughts, feeling detached from your own body. This is the stuff that makes you think you're going crazy.
  • Social Isolation: You can't relate to anyone anymore. Friends don't understand, family thinks you're sick. You pull away because it's too much to be around people.

This phase can last for years, honestly. It's not a quick fix. It's a deep, deep purge. And it's terrifying because it feels so out of control. You're no longer the one driving; the energy is. And it's taking you places you never wanted to go. It's forcing you to confront every single shadow, every wound, every lie you've ever told yourself. It's stripping away your ego, your identity, everything you thought you were. That's why it feels like death. Because in a way, it is. The old you is dying to make way for the new.

I remember lying on the floor, shaking, convinced I was having a heart attack, or a stroke, or both. Doctors ran tests, found nothing. They offered antidepressants, anti-anxiety meds. I tried some, they dulled the edge, but they didn't stop the internal earthquake. I just kept thinking, "This can't be what spiritual awakening is about." But it was. Or, at least, it was a very necessary part of it. If you're going through this, you need support. I found immense help in books like The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). She gets it. She really does.

Surviving the Dark Night

So, you're in the thick of it. What do you do? How do you survive when every fiber of your being is screaming for it to stop?

Acceptance and Surrender

This is probably the hardest part. Your ego wants to fight it, control it, make it go away. But you can't. The more you resist, the more intense it often gets. You have to learn to surrender to the process. To trust, somehow, that there's a deeper intelligence at work, even when it feels like chaos. It's like being caught in a powerful river. You can fight the current and drown, or you can let go and trust it to carry you. That doesn't mean you just lie there and do nothing, though. It means you stop fighting the internal experience itself. You acknowledge it, you feel it, but you don't judge it or try to push it away. This doesn't make it instantly better, but it stops you from making it worse.

For me, this meant literally saying out loud, "Okay, I give up. Do what you need to do." It wasn't a choice; it was exhaustion. But it was also a turning point. It's counterintuitive, I know. Everything in you wants to make the bad feelings stop. But when you stop fighting them, when you just allow them to be there, something subtle shifts. They don't disappear, but their grip loosens. I also found comfort in books like The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link), which helped me understand that these extreme states aren't necessarily mental illness, but often part of a spiritual emergence.

Practical Steps for Grounding

While you can't stop the process, you can absolutely help yourself manage the symptoms. Think of it like this: you're undergoing major internal surgery. You need to support your body and mind through it. Here's what helped me:

  • Grounding Practices: Get outside. Walk barefoot on the earth. Hug a tree. Spend time in nature. Eat root vegetables. Take salt baths. Anything that connects you to the physical world and helps dissipate excess energy.
  • Gentle Movement: Yoga, tai chi, walking, swimming. Avoid intense, stimulating exercise. The goal is to move energy, not pump yourself up.
  • Diet: Eat clean, whole foods. Avoid stimulants like caffeine, sugar, and alcohol. Your nervous system is already overwhelmed; don't add to it. Hydrate.
  • Sleep: This is a big one. It's hard when anxiety is through the roof, but prioritize sleep. Create a dark, quiet, cool space. Avoid screens before bed.
  • Creative Expression: Write, draw, paint, play music. Get that intense internal experience out of you in a constructive way. It's a powerful release.
  • Seek Support: This is crucial. Find someone who understands, even if it's just one person. A therapist who is open to spiritual emergence, a spiritual teacher, a support group. You can't do this alone. I wish I had known about resources like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) during my darkest times; it would have made a huge difference.
  • Patience: This isn't a race. It's a marathon. Be kind to yourself. There will be good days and bad days. Don't expect a quick fix.

Look, I know how isolating this can feel. You're probably scared to talk about it because you think people will judge you, or worse, commit you. But you need to find your tribe, even if it's online. There are others who've walked this path. They'll get it. They'll tell you you're not crazy. That validation is everything when you're feeling utterly lost. Trust me on that one.

The Other Side: What's Left After the Storm

So yeah, it feels like it'll never end, doesn't it? Like you're stuck in this hell forever. But it does change. It really does. The energy eventually integrates. The purging slows down. You start to find your footing again, but you're not the same person who started the journey. You're at its core different.

What's left after the storm? Well, a lot. You're often left with:

  • deep Peace: Not the hyped-up bliss of the honeymoon, but a deep, abiding inner peace that isn't dependent on external circumstances.
  • Authenticity: You can't pretend anymore. You've been stripped bare, and what's left is your true self. You're more honest, more real.
  • Compassion: For yourself, for others. You've been through hell, so you understand suffering in a way you never could before.
  • Wisdom: You've learned lessons that no book or teacher could ever give you. You have an intuitive understanding of life and consciousness.
  • Increased Resilience: After going through something like this, you realize how strong you actually are. You can face almost anything.
  • True Connection: The initial connection was fleeting; the one you find after integration is stable and real, a deep knowing of your place in the universe.

It's not that you'll never feel pain or sadness again. That's just part of being human. But your relationship to those feelings changes. You don't get swallowed by them in the same way. You have a deeper foundation, an inner strength that wasn't there before. The goal isn't to be constantly blissful; it's to be fully present, fully alive, and fully yourself, whatever that looks like in any given moment.

The honeymoon phase is just the appetizer. It's the universe's way of getting your attention, showing you a glimpse of what's possible, before it takes you on the hardest, most rewarding journey of your life. If you're in the middle of the crisis, I know it feels endless. But it's not. Keep going. Keep breathing. Keep reaching out. You're stronger than you think. And you're not alone. There's a whole community of us who've been through it and come out the other side. You can too. I've personally found great insights in The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link), which offers a clinical yet compassionate view of the process.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
